reason: Standalone observational joke about driving; no solicitation, contact channel, or charge.
One-line joke about tattoos; no solicitation. Held-out row, flagged only.
Divorce Barbie joke; self-contained punchline with no commercial mechanism.
Child-versus-teenager joke; conversational, no action requested.
Recipient asking what to do after a prize message; a victim query, not a solicitation. Held-out row, flagged only.
Personal reply from a named sender about a debt collector; no solicitation.
British joke: a dartboard with no doubles or trebles is useless. No real transaction intent or contact detail.
Stolen police toilet pun; no commercial content.
Personal question about having received dating spam; discussing spam is not sending it.
Personal rant about a disputed mobile bill; complaint, not solicitation.
Quotes a scam opener in order to warn the recipient about it; meta-warning, not the scam.
Reads as the user reporting money lost to shortcode 88066 and asking for help.
Near-duplicate of T2172; same help request about shortcode 88066.
Commentary on network billing responsibility; no solicitation. The 0A$NETWORKS prefix remains unexplained.
Complaint about receiving paid ringtone texts. Only one automated signal opposes; retained on policy reading and consistency with T1798 and T4296.
Flirtatious opener with no price, shortcode, or premium number. Personal message and adult-chat lure are both consistent with the text.
070 number with no tariff disclosure. UK 070 is both a personal follow-me range and a range abused by premium redirect scams; judged on the same standard as T3390.
Missed-call alert quoting a 070 number without tariff disclosure; same standard as T2710.
The ROMCAPspam prefix may be residue of a real horoscope campaign or a corpus annotation artifact; the body itself is ordinary warm chat.
Truncated text implying adult content, but no tariff, number, or shortcode survives in the row.
Requests address and date of birth for a product trial. Genuine recruitment by an acquaintance and cold data collection are both readable.
Club ticket notice; spam status depends on whether the recipient is a member who opted into club messages.
UNICEF disaster appeal. Charity solicitation is a genuine policy carve-out question, not a commercial broadcast.
Personal web page shared with a request for opinions. Depends on sender relationship; no charge or commercial mechanism. The 1/1 marker indicates a single-part message, not a chain.
Meta commentary demonstrating an anonymous SMS service to the recipient; sender intent cannot be recovered.
Service onboarding prompt asking for age and gender. Cannot distinguish a subscription the user initiated from a cold enrolment lure.
Truncated fragment that reads as a personal thank-you; too little text survives to decide.
Picture link with no tariff. A legitimate photo-sharing notification and a marketing link are both consistent.
Teaser campaign with a URL but no price, number, or reply keyword; commercial intent is implied rather than evidenced.
"I liked the new mobile" is ordinary ham. Word and character probabilities straddle 0.5 and neighbours are mixed, but predictive uncertainty alone is neither label error nor semantic ambiguity.
"Waiting for your call." is ordinary ham. The word model scores it 0.689 on shallow lexical overlap with call-to-action spam; no neighbour opposes the label.
The affectionate wording is packaging; "one gbp/sms" is an explicit per-message charge.
Quiz requiring a keyword reply follows the corpus premium-quiz pattern, though no price is shown.
"brought to you by GMW Ltd." is a bulk-advertising footer, although the row is truncated.
Prescription-drug advert disguised as an email alert; "call 123" carries no tariff.
Burger King promotion. A lawful advertiser still sends unsolicited commercial broadcast; there is no consent story available.
Interflora order deadline promotion with an 0800 number; unsolicited commercial broadcast.
WAP password notice bound to a subscription service at mobsi.com.
Explicit adult-image solicitation.
"cst std ntwk chg" plus GBP1.50 is a tariff disclosure.
Explicit content with a number and the Nikiyu4.net domain implying a commercial adult service, though no price is stated.
Promises later access to adult parties; solicitation without tariff disclosure.
"Txt XXX SLO(4msgs)" is a standard paid adult-SMS instruction.
Charge confirmation plus an upsell to send more paid content.
Adult site with credentials; content evidence is clear but no price is quoted.
Lucozade competition with a shortcode and 25p tariff.
Exact duplicate text of T3947.
Fragment 2/2 with a 150p tariff token; text is unreadable but all five neighbours are spam.
Dogging solicitation with a premium number and per-minute tariff.
Flirtatious packaging with explicit send and receive charges.
Ringtone shortcode fragment; all five neighbours are spam.
Cinema promotion with a shortcode and a reply keyword for more offers.
Explicit solicitation with shortcode and per-message tariff.
Premium quiz format with a reply shortcode.
Auction outbid notification with an opt-out keyword; standard bulk service spam.
Travel promotion with price and booking number.
Paid-content reminder; automated bulk service message.
Dating enrolment with a 100p per SMS tariff.
Ringtone advertisement fragment with a reply keyword.
Same enrolment pattern as H0151 with a 150p tariff.
Weekly ringtone subscription notification.
Bulk content promotion with a download link.
Loan advertisement with a company name and phone number.
Explicit adult line with a per-minute tariff.
Subscription top-up notice; automated bulk message.
Ringtone delivery confirmation with terms-and-conditions pointer.
Cash giveaway promotion with a site address.
Auction bid notice with an opt-out keyword and customer-care number.
WAP game promotion with charge notice and terms.
Exact duplicate text of T1427.
Ringtone subscription advert with a shortcode and GBP3 charge.
Stock pump-and-dump promotion.
"PRIVATE! Your account statement" is a standard spam opener.
Sports quiz subscription with a reply keyword.
Premium-number secret-admirer scam with a 150p tariff.
Subscription advert with an 18p per text charge.
Dating line with a premium number.
Adult chat line with an explicit per-minute tariff.
Same explicit adult line as T3592 with a PO box added.

Legitimate apartment-price discussion is pushed to spam by eleven digits and three currency markers; this is a clear cue-induced false positive.
A ringtone-club solicitation has almost none of the usual numeric or contact cues so the shallow model misses clear spam that the text model detects.
A premium-rate adult-chat lure is recovered mainly from a telephone number and price marker while the full-text baseline remains below threshold.
Phone and reply-cost markers drive the shallow probability to nearly one even though the full-text baseline remains below threshold.
A mobile-club offer is missed by the shallow probe because it lacks the usual phone currency and promotional-token pattern.
A benign chain-message questionnaire contains many digits and a reply cue which causes a shallow false positive despite ordinary conversational context.
An informal message about a pay rise contains digits currency and uppercase text which pushes the shallow model over the spam threshold.
URL stop and price cues recover a genuine subscription spam message that the frozen full-text baseline narrowly misses.
The public spam label reads like an ordinary joke; a joint model miss does not isolate a shortcut and is better handled by label adjudication.
This appears to be a recipient reply rather than an originating promotion so the disagreement is likely label noise rather than shortcut evidence.
Recruitment advertising with a URL and contact request is policy-sensitive so calling the shallow prediction a false positive would overstate the evidence.
This message is an exact-text duplicate of H0949 and should not be counted as an independent shortcut example.
The free-SMS service message is both policy-sensitive and duplicated so it is retained for ambiguity review rather than shortcut ranking.
The quiz could be a paid service but lacks explicit price or contact evidence; two model misses do not identify a specific shortcut mechanism.
The generic horoscope-style wording has uncertain label semantics and no strong shallow-versus-text contrast.
The word call is polysemous but the full-text score is also near the boundary making this weaker than the retained benign hard negatives.
